**Lower Hutt** ([Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_language "Māori language"): *Te Awa Kairangi ki Tai*)[[9]](#cite_note-9) is a [city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_New_Zealand "List of cities in New Zealand") in the [Wellington Region](/wiki/Wellington_Region "Wellington Region") of New Zealand. Administered by the [Hutt City Council](/wiki/Hutt_City_Council "Hutt City Council"), it is one of the four cities that constitute the [Wellington metropolitan area](/wiki/Wellington#Wellington_metropolitan_area "Wellington").

It is New Zealand's [sixth most populous](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_New_Zealand "List of cities in New Zealand") city, with a population of 114,200.[[8]](#cite_note-NZ_population_data_2018-8) The total area administered by the council is 376.4 km2 (145 sq mi) around the lower half of the [Hutt Valley](/wiki/Hutt_Valley "Hutt Valley") and along the eastern shores of [Wellington Harbour](/wiki/Wellington_Harbour "Wellington Harbour"), of which 78.54 km2 (30 sq mi) is urban.[[7]](#cite_note-Area-7) It is separated from the city of Wellington by the harbour, and from [Upper Hutt](/wiki/Upper_Hutt "Upper Hutt") by the Taita Gorge.

Lower Hutt is unique among New Zealand cities, as the name of the council does not match the name of the city it governs. Special legislation[[10]](#cite_note-10) has since 1991 given the council the name "Hutt City Council", while the name of the place itself remains "Lower Hutt City". This name has led to confusion, as Upper Hutt is administered by a separate city council, the Upper Hutt City Council. The entire Hutt Valley includes both Lower and Upper Hutt cities. Lower Hutt is also often simply called "the Hutt".

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lower_Hutt&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Before European settlement, thick forest covered most of the Hutt Valley, with areas of marshland close to the river's mouth. [Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_people "Māori people") inhabited the shoreline, with a [pā](/wiki/P%C4%81 "Pā") at each end of Petone beach.[[11]](#cite_note-history-11)

Māori welcomed the arrival of the [New Zealand Company](/wiki/New_Zealand_Company "New Zealand Company") ship [*Tory*](/wiki/New_Zealand_Company_ships#Tory "New Zealand Company ships") in 1839, and [William Wakefield](/wiki/William_Wakefield "William Wakefield") (the company's agent) negotiated with some local chiefs to allow settlement. The first immigrant ship, the *Aurora*, arrived on 22 January 1840, an event still commemorated every year on the Monday closest as [Wellington's Anniversary Day](/wiki/Wellington_Province#Anniversary_Day "Wellington Province"). A settlement, Britannia, grew up close to the mouth of the Hutt River ([Te Awa Kairangi](/wiki/Te_Awa_Kairangi_/_Hutt_River "Te Awa Kairangi / Hutt River") in Māori language), and settlers set up New Zealand's first newspaper and bank.[[11]](#cite_note-history-11)

The city takes its name from the English name given to the river, named after one of the founding members, director and chairman of the New Zealand Company, Sir [William Hutt](/wiki/William_Hutt_(British_MP) "William Hutt (British MP)"). The dual name of Hutt River Te Awa Kairangi has been used since 2010.[[12]](#cite_note-river-12)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Christ_Church_Taita.JPG/250px-Christ_Church_Taita.JPG)](/wiki/File:Christ_Church_Taita.JPG)

[Christ Church, Taitā](/wiki/Christ_Church,_Taita "Christ Church, Taita"), built in 1853 – the oldest extant church in the Wellington region

Within weeks of settlement the Hutt River flooded, and in March 1840 the majority of Britannia settlers decided to move to [Thorndon](/wiki/Thorndon,_New_Zealand "Thorndon, New Zealand"), (as of 2013[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Lower_Hutt&action=edit) in the heart of Wellington city), though some settlers remained at the north end of the harbour.[[11]](#cite_note-history-11) In the 1840s an area on the west bank of the Hutt River, in what is now [Alicetown](/wiki/Alicetown "Alicetown"), formed the village then known as Aglionby.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

In 1846 [conflict arose between European settlers and Māori](/wiki/Hutt_Valley_Campaign "Hutt Valley Campaign"), which led to armed conflict in the [Hutt Valley Campaign](/wiki/Hutt_Valley_Campaign "Hutt Valley Campaign").[[11]](#cite_note-history-11)

The [1855 Wairarapa earthquake](/wiki/1855_Wairarapa_earthquake "1855 Wairarapa earthquake") (in the range of magnitude 8.1 to 8.3) raised part of the lower valley, allowing reclamation of land from swamp. The fault escarpment from the earthquake is still visible, notably at Hutt Central School.[[11]](#cite_note-history-11)

On New Year's Day 1859 the first permanent lighthouse to be built in New Zealand was lit at Pencarrow Head. New Zealand's only female lighthouse keeper, [Mary Jane Bennett](/wiki/Mary_Jane_Bennett "Mary Jane Bennett"), became the inaugural operator of the lighthouse.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

The railway line from central Wellington reached Lower Hutt station (subsequently [Western Hutt](/wiki/Western_Hutt_railway_station "Western Hutt railway station")) in April 1874, with the line running north up the west side of the Hutt River to Silverstream opening two years later.[[11]](#cite_note-history-11)

Before the Second World War of 1939–1945, urban settlement in the lower Hutt Valley concentrated mainly on [Petone](/wiki/Petone "Petone"), central Lower Hutt and Eastbourne, with a total population of 30,000. In 1927 the [Public Works Department](/wiki/New_Zealand_Ministry_of_Works "New Zealand Ministry of Works") completed the construction of a branch railway line to [Waterloo](/wiki/Waterloo_railway_station,_Lower_Hutt "Waterloo railway station, Lower Hutt") on the east side of the river; the route diverging from the main line between Lower Hutt and Petone. Two years later the railway workshops moved from Petone to a [new larger site off the new branch at Woburn](/wiki/Hutt_Workshops "Hutt Workshops").

In the late 1940s new suburbs of [state housing](/wiki/State_housing_in_New_Zealand "State housing in New Zealand") developed along the eastern side of the Hutt Valley, from [Waiwhetū](/wiki/Waiwhet%C5%AB "Waiwhetū") to [Taitā](/wiki/Tait%C4%81,_New_Zealand "Taitā, New Zealand") (then known as Taita), to alleviate nationwide housing shortages and to cater for the [booming population](/wiki/Baby_boom "Baby boom"). Between 1946 and 1954 the railway line from Waterloo extended through these new suburbs to [Haywards](/wiki/Haywards "Haywards"), becoming the main line in 1954 when the existing main line between Haywards and Melling closed. By the end of the 1950s, Lower Hutt had a population of 80,000.

The Hutt City Council comprises a mayor and 12 councillors. The current [mayor](/wiki/Mayor_of_Lower_Hutt "Mayor of Lower Hutt") is [Campbell Barry](/wiki/Campbell_Barry "Campbell Barry"), who was first elected in [2019](/wiki/2019_Hutt_City_Council_election "2019 Hutt City Council election") succeeding [Ray Wallace](/wiki/Ray_Wallace_(mayor) "Ray Wallace (mayor)"). At the time of his election, Barry was New Zealand's youngest ever mayor.[[15]](#cite_note-15) The city's six electoral wards (Northern, Western, Central, Eastern, Harbour and Wainuiomata) each elect one councillor, with the remaining six councillors elected at-large.

Neighbouring councils are [Wellington City Council](/wiki/Wellington_City_Council "Wellington City Council") (adjoining to the west), [Porirua](/wiki/Porirua "Porirua") City Council to the north, [Upper Hutt](/wiki/Upper_Hutt "Upper Hutt") City Council to the north-east and [South Wairarapa District](/wiki/South_Wairarapa_District "South Wairarapa District") Council to the east. The boundaries of the Lower Hutt city local body have evolved from a series of amalgamations and boundary changes over the years.

The [Hutt County Council](/wiki/Hutt_County_Council "Hutt County Council"), established in 1877, covered the region from Wellington's south coast up to [Waikanae](/wiki/Waikanae "Waikanae"), excluding the [Wellington City Council](/wiki/Wellington_City_Council "Wellington City Council") area.[[11]](#cite_note-history-11) As the region grew, urban parts of the Hutt County became autonomous boroughs: [Petone](/wiki/Petone "Petone") in 1888, Lower Hutt in 1891, [Eastbourne](/wiki/Eastbourne,_New_Zealand "Eastbourne, New Zealand") in 1906, [Johnsonville](/wiki/Johnsonville,_New_Zealand "Johnsonville, New Zealand") in 1908, [Upper Hutt](/wiki/Upper_Hutt "Upper Hutt") in 1908, [Porirua](/wiki/Porirua "Porirua") in 1962 and [Kapiti](/wiki/Kapiti_Coast_(district),_New_Zealand "Kapiti Coast (district), New Zealand") in 1974.[[11]](#cite_note-history-11) In 1941 Lower Hutt became a city.[[11]](#cite_note-history-11) It incorporated [Normandale](/wiki/Normandale,_New_Zealand "Normandale, New Zealand") in 1957.

In 1987–1989 the New Zealand Government forced local authorities to consolidate, which led to Lower Hutt amalgamating with the adjacent Boroughs of Petone and Eastbourne and with the [Wainuiomata](/wiki/Wainuiomata "Wainuiomata") District (which had its independence for barely a year), and to the abolition of the Hutt County Council.

### National

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lower_Hutt&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: National")]

The area of Lower Hutt is covered by two [general electorates](/wiki/New_Zealand_electorates "New Zealand electorates") ([Hutt South](/wiki/Hutt_South "Hutt South") and [Remutaka](/wiki/Remutaka_(New_Zealand_electorate) "Remutaka (New Zealand electorate)")) and by two [Māori electorates](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_electorates "Māori electorates") ([Ikaroa-Rāwhiti](/wiki/Ikaroa-R%C4%81whiti "Ikaroa-Rāwhiti") in the north and [Te Tai Tonga](/wiki/Te_Tai_Tonga "Te Tai Tonga") in the south).[[17]](#cite_note-17)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lower_Hutt&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Geography")]

The city centres on the lower southern valley of the [Hutt River](/wiki/Hutt_River,_New_Zealand "Hutt River, New Zealand"), to the northeast of the city of [Wellington](/wiki/Wellington "Wellington"). The valley widens into a [delta](/wiki/River_delta "River delta") as the river nears its mouth, so the central urban area of the city forms a triangle with its longest side along the shoreline. In the upper reaches of the city the Western and Eastern Hutt Hills become closer, culminating in the Taitā Gorge at the northern end of Lower Hutt, separating the city from neighbouring Upper Hutt.[[18]](#cite_note-Geography-18)

Lower Hutt includes the cluster of small settlements that extend down the eastern coast of [Wellington Harbour](/wiki/Wellington_Harbour "Wellington Harbour"). These include the two large [townships](/wiki/Township "Township") of [Wainuiomata](/wiki/Wainuiomata "Wainuiomata") and [Eastbourne](/wiki/Eastbourne,_New_Zealand "Eastbourne, New Zealand"). The city also includes a large area of sparsely populated land to the east of the harbour, extending to Pencarrow Head and into the [Remutaka Range](/wiki/Remutaka_Range "Remutaka Range").[[18]](#cite_note-Geography-18) Lower Hutt's boundaries also include the islands in Wellington Harbour, the largest of which is [Matiu / Somes Island](/wiki/Matiu_/_Somes_Island "Matiu / Somes Island").[[18]](#cite_note-Geography-18)

### Te Awa Kairangi / Hutt River

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lower_Hutt&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Te Awa Kairangi / Hutt River")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Te_Awa_Kairangi_Hutt_River_Looking_downstream.jpg/250px-Te_Awa_Kairangi_Hutt_River_Looking_downstream.jpg)](/wiki/File:Te_Awa_Kairangi_Hutt_River_Looking_downstream.jpg)

Te Awa Kairangi Hutt River Looking downstream

A single major aquifer dominates the lower Hutt Valley: the [river](/wiki/Te_Awa_Kairangi_/_Hutt_River "Te Awa Kairangi / Hutt River"), originally named Heretaunga, and since 2010 known as "Te Awa Kairangi / Hutt River".[[18]](#cite_note-Geography-18)[[11]](#cite_note-history-11)[[12]](#cite_note-river-12) *Awakairangi* in the [Māori language](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_language "Māori language") means "river of food from the sky".

Lower Hutt occupies the lower regions of the [flood plain](/wiki/Floodplain "Floodplain") of the river, one of the most significant features of the city.[[18]](#cite_note-Geography-18) In the 20th century the Hutt River Board built [stopbanks](/wiki/Stopbank "Stopbank") to contain the river,[[20]](#cite_note-20) but the threat of flooding from heavy rainfall persists. In 1985 the river burst its banks, and since then floods have been on a smaller scale. Smaller streams and storm-water drains have also caused occasional problems when rainfall exceeds average levels.

Much of the land adjacent to the river is protected as reserve by the City Council and managed by the [Greater Wellington Regional Council](/wiki/Greater_Wellington_Regional_Council "Greater Wellington Regional Council") to provide popular recreational areas.[[18]](#cite_note-Geography-18) From the river mouth, walking and cycling trails and grassed areas occur at various points along both sides of the river up the [Hutt Valley](/wiki/Hutt_Valley "Hutt Valley") to [Te Mārua](/wiki/Te_M%C4%81rua "Te Mārua"), 28 km further north in Upper Hutt.

With lower river-levels in mid-summer, [algal blooms](/wiki/Algal_bloom "Algal bloom") have contributed to making slow-flowing areas anoxic. The Regional Council has cited the algal blooms as the cause of death of a small number of dogs swimming in the river, as well as of skin reactions in the case of swimmers.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)

Seven bridges cross the Hutt River within the city of Lower Hutt, with several other bridges built and replaced from the 1850s onwards.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

Tributaries of the Hutt River within Lower Hutt include:

* [Te Mome Stream](/w/index.php?title=Te_Mome_Stream&action=edit&redlink=1 "Te Mome Stream (page does not exist)")
* [Opahu Stream (Black Creek)](/w/index.php?title=Opahu_Stream_(Black_Creek)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Opahu Stream (Black Creek) (page does not exist)")[[24]](#cite_note-24)

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lower_Hutt&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Demographics")]

Lower Hutt City covers 376.40 km2 (145.33 sq mi)[[7]](#cite_note-Area-7) and had an estimated population of 114,200 as of June 2025,[[8]](#cite_note-NZ_population_data_2018-8) with a population density of 303 people per km2.

The city's population has remained stable from the 1990s to the 2013 census.[[26]](#cite_note-DPE-29) In the five years between the 2013 and 2018 censuses, the population grew steadily across the city with an increasing amount of homes being purchased and the area seen as more affordable comparable to the rest of the region.[[27]](#cite_note-30)

Lower Hutt City had a population of 107,562 in the [2023 New Zealand census](/wiki/2023_New_Zealand_census "2023 New Zealand census"), an increase of 3,030 people (2.9%) since the [2018 census](/wiki/2018_New_Zealand_census "2018 New Zealand census"), and an increase of 9,324 people (9.5%) since the [2013 census](/wiki/2013_New_Zealand_census "2013 New Zealand census"). There were 53,082 males, 54,009 females and 468 people of [other genders](/wiki/Non-binary_gender "Non-binary gender") in 39,279 dwellings.[[30]](#cite_note-33) 3.8% of people identified as [LGBTIQ+](/wiki/LGBTQ "LGBTQ"). The median age was 37.5 years (compared with 38.1 years nationally). There were 20,595 people (19.1%) aged under 15 years, 19,896 (18.5%) aged 15 to 29, 51,261 (47.7%) aged 30 to 64, and 15,807 (14.7%) aged 65 or older.[[29]](#cite_note-Census_2023-32)

[New Zealand Sign Language](/wiki/New_Zealand_Sign_Language "New Zealand Sign Language") was known by 0.6%. The percentage of people born overseas was 27.6, compared with 28.8% nationally.

Religious affiliations were 33.9% [Christian](/wiki/Christianity_in_New_Zealand "Christianity in New Zealand"), 4.5% [Hindu](/wiki/Hinduism_in_New_Zealand "Hinduism in New Zealand"), 1.4% [Islam](/wiki/Islam_in_New_Zealand "Islam in New Zealand"), 1.1% [Māori religious beliefs](/wiki/Religion_of_M%C4%81ori_people "Religion of Māori people"), 1.3% [Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhism_in_New_Zealand "Buddhism in New Zealand"), 0.4% [New Age](/wiki/New_Age "New Age"), 0.1% [Jewish](/wiki/Judaism_in_New_Zealand "Judaism in New Zealand"), and 1.8% other religions. People who answered that they had [no religion](/wiki/Irreligion_in_New_Zealand "Irreligion in New Zealand") were 49.6%, and 6.1% of people did not answer the census question.

Of those at least 15 years old, 19,188 (22.1%) people had a bachelor's or higher degree, 42,231 (48.6%) had a post-high school certificate or diploma, and 20,148 (23.2%) people exclusively held high school qualifications. The median income was $47,800, compared with $41,500 nationally. 13,125 people (15.1%) earned over $100,000 compared to 12.1% nationally. The employment status of those at least 15 was that 48,201 (55.4%) people were employed full-time, 10,395 (12.0%) were part-time, and 2,694 (3.1%) were unemployed.[[29]](#cite_note-Census_2023-32)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lower_Hutt&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Economy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/Gear_Meat_Company%2C_Petone_%2821430987361%29.jpg/250px-Gear_Meat_Company%2C_Petone_%2821430987361%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gear_Meat_Company,_Petone_(21430987361).jpg)

Early 1900s photo taken from Korokoro looking down on Petone and the Gear Meat Company

Historically, Petone, Seaview and Gracefield have been the main area for industry in the Wellington region, with industries including meat processing and freezing, motor vehicle assembly, and timber processing. As businesses have taken advantage of global manufacturing efficiencies, much of this large scale industry has changed to smaller design-led and medium-sized industries exporting to the world. Over the past 25 years service, distribution, and consumer-oriented sectors have increased. Lower Hutt remains the main location for light industrial activity in the Wellington Region.

Until post-war housing development took over, the central and northern areas of the city were largely market gardens.

In 2010 the lower reaches of the [Waiwhetū Stream](/wiki/Waiwhet%C5%AB_Stream "Waiwhetū Stream") was cleaned up to remove toxins from decades of industry use. The channel was also widened to better protect against floods and native plantings and management has seen native waterlife and birds return to their habitat.[[40]](#cite_note-43)

Petone's Jackson Street and neighbouring areas have seen a resurgence in to one of Wellington's most popular retail and hospitality areas.

Lower Hutt has one of the greatest proportions of science, technology and high value manufacturing businesses in New Zealand. [Crown Research Institute](/wiki/Crown_Research_Institute "Crown Research Institute") GNS Science and New Zealand's innovation centre and business accelerator [Callaghan Innovation](/wiki/Callaghan_Innovation "Callaghan Innovation") are based in Lower Hutt, along with over 800 research organisations in high-end manufacturing, research and technology.[[41]](#cite_note-44)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Avalon_television_studios%2C_Lower_Hutt%2C_NZ_%28cropped%29.JPG/250px-Avalon_television_studios%2C_Lower_Hutt%2C_NZ_%28cropped%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Avalon_television_studios,_Lower_Hutt,_NZ_(cropped).JPG)

Avalon TV Studios from Harcourt Werry Drive

The suburb of [Avalon](/wiki/Avalon,_New_Zealand "Avalon, New Zealand") was home to New Zealand's television industry from 1975 until the late 1980s. The [Avalon film and television studios](/wiki/Avalon,_New_Zealand#Avalon_Studios "Avalon, New Zealand") were New Zealand's first purpose-built television studios, and Avalon is the largest television studio complex in Australasia. The studios were home to [Television One](/wiki/TV_One_(New_Zealand) "TV One (New Zealand)") from 1975 to 1980, when it merged with [South Pacific Television](/wiki/South_Pacific_Television "South Pacific Television") to form [Television New Zealand](/wiki/Television_New_Zealand "Television New Zealand") (TVNZ). After 1989 most of TVNZ's operations moved to Auckland, and the studios were eventually sold off in 2012 to a consortium of Wellington investors.[[42]](#cite_note-Stuff.co.nz_4868116-45) Avalon continues to operate independently with seven film and television studios used primarily as a feature film production base.

A large proportion of Lower Hutt's residents commute to the mainly commercial, service and government offices in Wellington City 12 km to the south-west.

## Culture and leisure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lower_Hutt&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Culture and leisure")]

[![Empty children's swimming pool](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Huia_Pool%2C_Lower_Hutt.jpg/250px-Huia_Pool%2C_Lower_Hutt.jpg)](/wiki/File:Huia_Pool,_Lower_Hutt.jpg)

Children's pool at Huia Pool + Fitness

Several education and research facilities of national significance are in the southern half of the city. Cultural facilities include the [Petone Settlers Museum](/wiki/Petone_Settlers_Museum "Petone Settlers Museum"), [War Memorial Library](/wiki/Lower_Hutt_War_Memorial_Library "Lower Hutt War Memorial Library"), [Dowse Art Museum](/wiki/Dowse_Art_Museum "Dowse Art Museum") and [Vogel House](/wiki/Vogel_House "Vogel House").[[43]](#cite_note-46)

The city possesses civic administration buildings constructed in the 1950s that are regarded as representative architecture of the era. A building of national significance is [Vogel House](/wiki/Vogel_House "Vogel House"), a two-storey wooden residence that was the official residence of the [Prime Minister of New Zealand](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_New_Zealand "Prime Minister of New Zealand") for much of the 20th century. It is a prime example of early colonial architecture in New Zealand and operates today as a tourist attraction.

The city is popular for outdoor sports, especially mountain biking, hiking, recreational walking and fishing.[[44]](#cite_note-47) The central city is home to [Queensgate Shopping Centre](/wiki/Queensgate_Shopping_Centre,_New_Zealand "Queensgate Shopping Centre, New Zealand"), the largest shopping centre in the lower North Island.[[45]](#cite_note-48) The Riverbank car park adjacent to the central city is home to a Saturday produce market.[[46]](#cite_note-49)[[47]](#cite_note-50)

Among the filming locations for [*The Lord of the Rings*](/wiki/The_Lord_of_the_Rings_(film_series) "The Lord of the Rings (film series)") directed by [Peter Jackson](/wiki/Peter_Jackson "Peter Jackson"), Dry Creek quarry, which dominates the hills above the suburb of Taitā, became the site for a huge medieval castle built for scenes of Helm's Deep and Minas Tirith.[[48]](#cite_note-51)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lower_Hutt&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Education")]

See also: [List of schools in the Wellington Region § Lower Hutt City](/wiki/List_of_schools_in_the_Wellington_Region#Lower_Hutt_City "List of schools in the Wellington Region")

Lower Hutt has four state secondary schools: [Taita College](/wiki/Taita_College "Taita College"), [Naenae College](/wiki/Naenae_College "Naenae College"), [Hutt Valley High School](/wiki/Hutt_Valley_High_School "Hutt Valley High School") and [Wainuiomata High School](/wiki/Wainuiomata_High_School "Wainuiomata High School"). Other secondary schools include [Chilton Saint James School](/wiki/Chilton_Saint_James_School "Chilton Saint James School"), a private girls school; [Raphael House Rudolf Steiner School](/wiki/Raphael_House_Rudolf_Steiner_School "Raphael House Rudolf Steiner School"), a state integrated Waldorf education school; [Sacred Heart College](/wiki/Sacred_Heart_College,_Lower_Hutt "Sacred Heart College, Lower Hutt"), a state integrated Catholic girls school; [St Bernard's College](/wiki/St._Bernard%27s_College,_Lower_Hutt "St. Bernard's College, Lower Hutt"), a state integrated Catholic boys school; and [St Oran's College](/wiki/St_Oran%27s_College "St Oran's College"), a state integrated Presbyterian girls school.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Open_Polytechnic_Main_Entrance_%2815409927572%29.jpg/250px-Open_Polytechnic_Main_Entrance_%2815409927572%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Open_Polytechnic_Main_Entrance_(15409927572).jpg)

The main entrance to the Open Polytechnic of New Zealand

The city is home to two tertiary institutes: the [Wellington Institute of Technology](/wiki/Wellington_Institute_of_Technology "Wellington Institute of Technology") in Petone, and [The Open Polytechnic of New Zealand](/wiki/The_Open_Polytechnic_of_New_Zealand "The Open Polytechnic of New Zealand") in Waterloo.

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lower_Hutt&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Media")]

The city is served by [The Post](/wiki/The_Post_(New_Zealand_newspaper) "The Post (New Zealand newspaper)"), [The Hutt News](/wiki/The_Hutt_News "The Hutt News") and community newspapers.

Local [LPFM](/wiki/Low-power_broadcasting "Low-power broadcasting") radio station The Cheese broadcasts in the city, and is licensed with [APRA](/wiki/Australasian_Performing_Right_Association "Australasian Performing Right Association") and [PPNZ](/wiki/PPNZ "PPNZ") in New Zealand for music broadcasting rights. It began Saturday 1 July 2006 and its original frequency was at 88.4 FM from [Wainuiomata](/wiki/Wainuiomata "Wainuiomata"). The station can be heard in Lower Hutt on 87.9 MHz FM with simulcasting via their official website. Subscribers to Vodafone TV can hear The Cheese on channel 417. The station broadcasts a mix of music from the 80s, 90s, recent and current hits.[[49]](#cite_note-52)[[50]](#cite_note-53)

## Flora and fauna

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lower_Hutt&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Flora and fauna")]

Hills to about 350 m (1000 ft) line both sides of the valley within the city limits. The western hills have been populated as residential areas, but the eastern side is protected and clad in native [bush and scrub](/wiki/Forest "Forest"), and the ubiquitous [gorse](/wiki/Gorse "Gorse") in areas that have been cleared as a result of scrub fires or earlier human activity.

Native birds are common, including the [New Zealand pigeon](/wiki/New_Zealand_pigeon "New Zealand pigeon"), [tūī](/wiki/T%C5%AB%C4%AB "Tūī"), [grey fantail](/wiki/Grey_fantail "Grey fantail"), [silvereye](/wiki/Silvereye "Silvereye"), [shining cuckoo](/wiki/Shining_cuckoo "Shining cuckoo") (in season), [grey warbler](/wiki/Grey_warbler "Grey warbler") and [morepork](/wiki/Morepork "Morepork"). Introduced species include the [common blackbird](/wiki/Common_blackbird "Common blackbird"), [song thrush](/wiki/Song_thrush "Song thrush"), [house sparrow](/wiki/House_sparrow "House sparrow"), [European goldfinch](/wiki/European_goldfinch "European goldfinch"), [common chaffinch](/wiki/Common_chaffinch "Common chaffinch"), [common starling](/wiki/Common_starling "Common starling"), and [Australian magpie](/wiki/Australian_magpie "Australian magpie").
